title: FAQs - FOMC Economic Projections
summary: This Federal Reserve FAQ page explains how the FOMC collects and publishes economic projections (Summary of Economic Projections) from Board members and Reserve Bank presidents four times per year in March, June, September, and December. The projections include real GDP growth, unemployment rate, PCE inflation, and core PCE inflation forecasts, as well as participants' views on the appropriate target federal funds rate path.
key_points: Projections are collected from all Board Governors and Federal Reserve Bank presidents four times yearly at March, June, September, and December FOMC meetings.
Each policymaker provides forecasts for real GDP, unemployment rate, PCE inflation, and core PCE inflation, measured Q4-to-Q4.
Since January 2012, projections include each policymaker's estimate of appropriate federal funds rate at end of current year, subsequent years, and longer run.
March and June projections cover current year, next two years, and longer run; September and December extend to three years forward.
The range includes all projections; central tendency excludes three highest and three lowest projections.
Initial projections are due the Friday before the meeting; revisions allowed until the morning of the second meeting day.
why_it_matters: This FAQ provides foundational context for understanding the Federal Reserve's Summary of Economic Projections (SEP), commonly known as the 'dot plot.' Market participants rely on SEP releases to gauge policymakers' views on appropriate monetary policy and economic trajectory. Knowing how to interpret ranges, central tendencies, and projection horizons is essential for following Fed communications.
macro_context: This page explains the mechanics of how FOMC projections are structured and collected, which informs how markets interpret subsequent projection releases. The longer-run projections represent estimates of potential GDP growth, natural unemployment rate, and the inflation target consistent with the Fed's dual mandate.
important_numbers: March, June, September, December (FOMC meeting months for projections)
